items.front: What is the purpose of ATIS under 2-9-1?
items.back: To provide advance noncontrol airport, terminal-area, and meteorological information to aircraft.

items.front: How is each ATIS message identified?
items.back: By a phonetic code word at both the beginning and the end of the message.

items.front: How are ATIS code words sequenced over time?
items.back: Sequentially from Alpha through Zulu, then repeated without regard to the beginning of a new day.

items.front: How is the first resumed ATIS message identified after an interruption of more than 12 hours?
items.back: With Alpha or the first assigned alphabet letter word.

items.front: What must be checked before an ATIS recording is transmitted?
items.back: Completeness, accuracy, speech rate, and proper enunciation.

items.front: What may separate arrival and departure ATIS broadcasts contain?
items.back: Only the information appropriate for that operation.

items.front: When is a new ATIS recording made because of weather?
items.back: Whenever any new official weather is received, even if the values do not change.

items.front: What braking-action change requires a new ATIS recording?
items.back: When braking action reports indicate braking is worse than what the current ATIS includes.

items.front: What other kinds of changes can trigger a new ATIS recording under 2-9-2?
items.back: Any other pertinent change, such as runway change, approach in use, or new or canceled NOTAM or PIREP information.

items.front: After a pilot acknowledges the ATIS, when may controllers omit items already contained in it?
items.back: When those items are current; rapidly changing conditions are still issued by ATC.

items.front: What should ATC do when a pilot does not state the appropriate ATIS code on initial contact?
items.back: Ask the pilot to confirm receipt of the current ATIS information.

items.front: What must be issued if a pilot cannot receive the ATIS?
items.back: The current weather, runway in use, approach or departure information, pertinent NOTAMs, and airport conditions.

items.front: What must be included when broadcasting an ATIS code change on frequency?
items.back: The change in code and the known pertinent operational information that caused the change.

items.front: What core items belong in an ATIS broadcast as appropriate?
items.back: Facility name, code letter, latest weather time, weather and altimeter, and the approach and runway in use.

items.front: Which weather observation remarks must always be included in the ATIS when present?
items.back: Lightning, cumulonimbus, and towering cumulus clouds.

items.front: How long must a reported unauthorized laser illumination event remain on the ATIS?
items.back: For one hour following the last report.

items.front: Under what condition may ceiling or sky condition, visibility, and obstructions to vision be omitted from the ATIS?
items.back: When the ceiling is above 5,000 feet and the visibility is more than 5 miles.

items.front: What warning phraseology is required when a runway length has been shortened?
items.back: Warning must preface the runway number, shortened must be included, and the available length in feet must be stated.

items.front: What statement is required when a FICON NOTAM shows runway condition codes X/X/X?
items.back: Runway Condition Codes Missing.

items.front: What statement is added when runway condition codes are 3/3/3 and a Slippery When Wet FICON NOTAM applies?
items.back: Slippery When Wet.

items.front: What must the ATIS tell pilots to do on initial contact regarding the broadcast?
items.back: Acknowledge receipt of the ATIS message on initial contact.
